sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 63-year-old male who has been experiencing a cough for approximately 3 months, which has been intermittent and has worsened with exercise, specifically while loading horses.
He had a chest X-ray that showed a lung nodule, which was further confirmed by a CT scan.
He was referred to our office for evaluation.
He has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
He stopped smoking 7 years ago and has no family history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
He was a previous smoker, having stopped 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilaterally, with no evidence of wheezing or crackles.
The heart is regular in rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities are pink, warm, and dry, with no edema.
The patient has a small amount of swelling in the knuckles, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: The CT scan shows a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth in appearance and does not show any evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Lung nodule, active; Rheumatoid arthritis, under control.
Plan: I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to assess the patient's lung function and determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will also schedule a PET CT to further evaluate the nodule.
I will recommend a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will involve three small incisions on the side of the right chest, general anesthesia, and a hospital stay of one night.
I will also instruct the patient to continue following up with their rheumatologist and maintaining their current medication therapy.
Follow-up: The patient will be discharged the next day after the procedure, with a follow-up appointment to be scheduled in a few minutes to review the results and discuss further management.
